blocks.content: In this lesson, students learn routine actions, days of the week, basic time, long answers with do and does, what and what time questions, and simple social expressions for favors, surprise, thanks, and asking what is going on.

messages.speaker: Emma
messages.text: Not much. I'm getting ready for school.

messages.speaker: Leo
messages.text: Really? What time do you go to school?

messages.speaker: Emma
messages.text: I go to school at eight o'clock.

messages.speaker: Emma
messages.text: Can you help me with my bag?

alternatives: Can you help me with this?

cards.backText: Use this to ask what is happening.
A simple answer when nothing special is happening.
Use these expressions to show surprise.
Use this to ask for a favor.
Use these expressions to accept a favor request.
Use these to thank someone and respond to thanks.

blocks.text:
A: What's [] on?
B: Not much.
A: Can you [] me?
B: Sure.
A: Thanks!
B: No [].

cards.backText: To eat in the middle of the day.

blocks.title: WHAT + DO + YOU + DO?
blocks.explanation: Use what to ask for information about an action or activity.
blocks.details: In this lesson, students use What do you do...? to ask about routines.

cells.text: What do you do in the morning?
What do you do after school?
What do you do at night?

blocks.instruction: Complete the questions with what or do.
blocks.text:
1. [] do you do in the morning?
2. What [] you do after school?
3. What do you [] at night?
4. [] do you do on Saturdays?

blocks.instruction: Create routine questions with what do you do.

examples.output: What do you do in the morning?

blocks.title: Your Routine
prompts: What do you do in the morning?
What do you do after school?
What do you do after work?
What do you do at night?
What do you do on Saturdays?

blocks.content: 4. Long Answers with Do and Does

blocks.title: SHORT ANSWER + LONG ANSWER
blocks.explanation: A short answer is good, but a long answer gives more information.
blocks.details: Keep the short answer first. Then add one simple sentence.

cells.text: Yes, I do. I study English at night.
No, I don't. I study full time.
Yes, he does. He wakes up at seven.
No, she doesn't. She goes to school.

blocks.text:
1. Do you study English?
Yes, I []. I study English at night.

2. Do you work?
No, I []. I go to school.

3. Does Leo wake up early?
Yes, he []. He wakes up at seven.

4. Does Emma work?
No, she []. She studies full time.

blocks.instruction: Turn the short answer into a long answer.

examples.output: Yes, I do. I study English at night.

items: Do you wake up early? / Yes
Do you work on Saturdays? / No
Does Emma study at night? / No
Does the restaurant open early? / Yes

blocks.content: Now students learn one important idea: when we talk about he, she, or it in simple sentences, the action usually changes a little.

blocks.title: HE / SHE / IT + ACTION + S
blocks.explanation: Use the simple action with I, you, we, and they. Add -s or -es with he, she, and it in affirmative sentences.
blocks.details: Keep this page simple. Do not overexplain spelling rules yet. Focus on common routine verbs.

blocks.text:
1. I wake up at seven. He [] up at seven.
2. I go to school. She [] to school.
3. They study English. He [] English.
4. We have lunch. She [] lunch.
5. I brush my teeth. He [] his teeth.

blocks.title: DOES QUESTIONS: NO -S ON THE ACTION
blocks.explanation: In questions with does, the action goes back to the simple form.
blocks.details: This prevents the common mistake: Does she studies?

blocks.title: On + Day
blocks.ruleContext: Use on before a day of the week.
blocks.expandableContent:
I study English on Mondays.
I work on Fridays.
She goes to school on weekdays.
He plays games on Saturdays.

blocks.text:
1. I study English [] Mondays.
2. She works [] Fridays.
3. He plays soccer [] Saturdays.
4. We go to school [] weekdays.

blocks.title: Basic Time
blocks.expandableContent:
1:00 = one o'clock
2:00 = two o'clock
7:00 = seven o'clock
10:00 = ten o'clock
12:00 = twelve o'clock

blocks.title: WHAT TIME + DO / DOES
blocks.explanation: Use what time to ask about the hour of an action.

cells.text: What time do you wake up?
I wake up at seven o'clock.
What time do you go to school?
I go to school at eight o'clock.
What time does she have lunch?
She has lunch at twelve o'clock.
What time does he go to bed?
He goes to bed at ten o'clock.

blocks.instruction: Complete with what time, do, does, or at.
blocks.text:
1. [] do you wake up?
2. What time [] you go to school?
3. What time [] she have lunch?
4. I wake up [] seven o'clock.
5. He goes to bed [] ten o'clock.

examples.output: What time do you wake up?

blocks.title: Ask About Time
prompts: What time do you wake up?
What time do you have breakfast?
What time do you go to school or work?
What time do you have dinner?
What time do you go to bed?

blocks.title: SOCIAL ENGLISH
blocks.explanation: Use these expressions in everyday situations.
blocks.details: These phrases are useful in class, at home, and at work.

messages.speaker: Bruno
messages.text: I'm studying for my English test.

details.label: Role
details.value: Ask what is going on, show surprise, and ask for a favor.

details.label: Role
details.value: Answer, accept the favor request, and respond to thanks.

blocks.tips: Use: What's going on? / Really? / Can you help me? / Sure. / Thanks. / You're welcome.

blocks.title: A Day in Leo's Life
blocks.text: Leo wakes up at seven o'clock. He has breakfast at seven thirty. He goes to school on Mondays, Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays, and Fridays. He studies English on Mondays and Wednesdays. After school, he goes home and does homework. At night, he has dinner with his family. He goes to bed at ten o'clock.

questions.question: What time does Leo wake up?
Does Leo go to school on Mondays?
What days does Leo study English?
What does Leo do after school?
questions.answer: He goes home and does homework.

questions.question: What time does Leo go to bed?

blocks.title: Listen: Emma's Routine
blocks.transcript: Emma wakes up at six o'clock. She takes a shower and has breakfast. She goes to school at eight o'clock. She studies English on Tuesdays and Thursdays. At night, she reads a book and goes to bed at ten o'clock.

transcriptHighlights.text: goes to school at eight o'clock

questions.question: What time does Emma wake up?
What time does Emma go to school?
What days does Emma study English?
What time does Emma go to bed?

details.label: Mission
details.value: Interview a classmate about routine, days, and time.

details.label: Mission
details.value: Answer with long answers and ask two questions back.

blocks.tips: Use What do you do...? / What time do you...? / Do you...? / Yes, I do. I... / No, I don't. I...

blocks.title: Final Speaking Round
prompts: What do you do in the morning?
What time do you wake up?
What time do you go to school or work?
What days do you study English?
Do you study English at night?
What does your friend do after school?
What time does your friend go to bed?
Ask your partner for one favor.

blocks.title: My Daily Routine
blocks.prompt: Write 7 to 9 short sentences about your routine. Include what you do in the morning, one time, one day of the week, one long answer with do, and one sentence about another person using he or she.

blocks.content: This page keeps the answer key inside the authoring JSON. Remove it from the public export if needed.

blocks.title: Answer Key
blocks.ruleContext: Core answers for the controlled activities in Lesson 3.
blocks.expandableContent:
Page 1 Fill: going, help, problem.
Page 3 Fill: What, do, do, What.
Page 4 Fill: do, don't, does, doesn't.
Page 5 Fill: wakes, goes, studies, has, brushes.
Page 6 Fill: on, on, on, on.
Page 7 Fill: o'clock, o'clock, o'clock, o'clock.
Page 8 Fill: What time, do, does, at, at.
Reading: seven o'clock; yes, he does; Mondays and Wednesdays; he goes home and does homework; ten o'clock.
Listening: six o'clock; eight o'clock; Tuesdays and Thursdays; ten o'clock.
